concept: Salmon / The Returning Fish / The River's Own Silver
description: The salmon: the great silver fish that leaves its river for the sea and returns, years later, to the very water that hatched it, leaping falls and weirs to get home. 'Shalumi' names the strongest homecoming in the animal world, and with it the health of the river itself: salmon live only in clean water, so their presence is the water's own testimony. Netted in season and left alone otherwise, the salmon is the meal a river gives a household that has kept it well.
sound_symbolism: The word opens on 'shalu' (fish) whole, placing the salmon inside its family the way 'tupiwa' (rabbit) grows from 'tupi' (jump). The soft fricative 'sh' is the water's own hush, the liquid 'l' its current, and the closing '-mi' is the light, quick ending it shares with 'loremi' (branch) and 'wakomi' (surprise): the flick of a body clearing a weir.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a noun. A counted salmon may take optional 'lipha': 'ta lipha shalumi' (one salmon); 'ta shalumi' is also grammatical. 'lo shalumi mua luphore wishe.' (The salmon swim in the river.) Its net is 'mera': 'lo miona roe mera shalumi kawepa.' (People catch salmon with nets.) Where 'shalu' names any fish, 'shalumi' is the specific silver returner.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A river with salmon in it is a river healed; the fish is the living proof that water was given back its life, the regeneration a whole society can read from a boat.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Salmon are taken in season, by net and weir, in quantities a village actually eats; the fish teaches sufficiency, since wanting salmon every day is how a river loses them.
